learning_objectives: Understand the stages of academic writing: prewriting, drafting, revising, editing
Distinguish academic writing from casual and professional writing
Identify the expectations of college-level writing
readings: They Say / I Say Ch. 1 by Graff & Birkenstein
The Elements of Style, Introduction and Ch. 1 by Strunk & White

lecture_notes.introduction: Academic writing is a conversation with a scholarly community. Every paper you write responds to existing ideas, proposes new ones, and supports claims with evidence. This course will teach you to join that conversation with confidence.
key_concepts: Writing as a process, not a product
Prewriting strategies: brainstorming, outlining, freewriting
The rhetorical situation: audience, purpose, context
Differences between academic, professional, and casual registers
Common expectations in college-level writing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparison of three versions of the same argument written for an academic journal, a business memo, and a social media post
setup_requirements: Word processor or Google Docs access

review.value: Prewriting (generating ideas) > Drafting (getting ideas on paper) > Revising (improving content and structure) > Editing (fixing grammar and mechanics)
Every piece of writing has an audience (who reads it), purpose (why it exists), and context (the circumstances surrounding it)

learning_objectives: Craft a clear, arguable thesis statement
Organize an essay using standard academic structure
readings: Writing Analytically Ch. 2 by Rosenwasser & Stephen

lecture_notes.introduction: A thesis statement is the backbone of an academic essay. It makes a specific, debatable claim that the rest of the paper supports with evidence and reasoning.
key_concepts: Characteristics of a strong thesis: specific, arguable, significant
The difference between a topic and a thesis
Essay structure: introduction, body paragraphs, conclusion
Topic sentences and paragraph unity
The funnel introduction and the mirror conclusion
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop transforming weak thesis statements into strong ones using student-generated topics

review.value: Not a fact ('The earth orbits the sun') or too broad ('Technology is important'). A strong thesis: 'Social media algorithms undermine democratic discourse by creating ideological echo chambers.'
Each paragraph makes one point (topic sentence), supports it with evidence, and explains how it connects to the thesis

learning_objectives: Develop effective search strategies for academic databases
Navigate the university library's digital resources
Distinguish between scholarly and popular sources
readings: The Craft of Research Ch. 3-4 by Booth, Colomb & Williams

lecture_notes.introduction: Research is not just Googling. Academic research requires systematic searching of scholarly databases using precise strategies to find credible, relevant sources.
key_concepts: Boolean operators: AND, OR, NOT
Keyword development and synonym expansion
Academic databases: JSTOR, Google Scholar, PubMed, EBSCO
Scholarly journals vs. magazines vs. newspapers vs. websites
The research question as a guide for source selection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live library database search demonstrating Boolean operators and filter techniques
setup_requirements: Access to library databases through campus network or VPN

review.value: AND narrows results (both terms required), OR broadens (either term), NOT excludes (removes a term)
Scholarly sources are peer-reviewed, written by experts, cite other research; popular sources target general audiences and may lack rigorous verification

class_schedule.topic: Integrating Sources: Summary, Paraphrase, and Quotation
learning_objectives: Summarize, paraphrase, and quote sources accurately
Integrate source material smoothly into your own writing

lecture_notes.introduction: Using sources is not just about finding them. The skill lies in weaving other people's ideas into your own argument while maintaining your voice and giving proper credit.
key_concepts: When to summarize, paraphrase, or quote directly
Signal phrases and attribution verbs
The quotation sandwich: introduce, quote, analyze
Patchwriting: the gray area between paraphrase and plagiarism
Synthesizing multiple sources around a single point
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students practice paraphrasing the same passage and compare results for accuracy and originality

review.value: Introduce the source and context, present the quotation, then analyze or explain how it supports your point
Replacing a few words in a source while keeping its structure is not paraphrasing; it is patchwriting and is a form of academic dishonesty

learning_objectives: Format citations correctly in APA and MLA styles
Create a properly formatted Works Cited or References page
Understand what constitutes plagiarism and how to avoid it
readings: APA Publication Manual, 7th Edition, selected sections

lecture_notes.introduction: Citations are not just formatting busywork. They are the mechanism by which academic writing maintains its credibility and allows readers to verify and build on your work.
key_concepts: Purpose of citation: credit, verification, and scholarly conversation
APA style: author-date in-text, References page
MLA style: author-page in-text, Works Cited page
When to cite: direct quotes, paraphrases, ideas, data, images
Self-plagiarism and common citation mistakes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on citation formatting exercise using Zotero or EasyBib
setup_requirements: Zotero or other citation manager installed

review.value: Author's last name and year: (Smith, 2023) or Smith (2023) argues that...
Author's last name and page number: (Smith 42) or Smith argues that '...' (42)

learning_objectives: Apply systematic revision strategies to improve draft quality
Provide constructive, specific feedback in peer review
readings: Revision Strategies That Teach Ch. 1-2 (provided excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Good writing is rewriting. Revision is where an adequate draft becomes a strong paper. This session focuses on systematic strategies for improving your own work and providing useful feedback to others.
key_concepts: Reverse outlining: mapping what each paragraph actually does
Higher-order concerns: thesis, evidence, structure, logic
Lower-order concerns: grammar, punctuation, formatting
Peer review guidelines: be specific, be constructive, address the writing not the writer
Reading aloud as a revision tool
lecture_notes.demonstration: Peer review workshop using a sample student essay with guided feedback form
setup_requirements: Draft of Essay 1 for peer review
Peer review feedback form (provided in LMS)

review.value: After drafting, summarize each paragraph in one sentence to check if the paper's actual structure matches the intended argument
Fix argument and structure first (higher-order), then grammar and mechanics (lower-order); polishing sentences in a paragraph that needs to be cut is wasted effort

quizzes.topic: Academic Writing Fundamentals and Research Strategies

questions.question: What makes a thesis statement 'arguable'? Provide one example of a non-arguable statement and revise it into an arguable thesis.
questions.answer: An arguable thesis makes a claim that reasonable people could disagree with, requiring evidence to support. A non-arguable statement: 'Many students use social media.' This is a fact, not debatable. Revised arguable thesis: 'Universities should ban social media use during lectures because it reduces attention spans and lowers exam performance by an average of 15%.' This is arguable because someone could dispute the policy, the causal link, or the significance of the data.

rubric.key: Strong revision that is genuinely arguable

questions.question: Explain how Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT) work in database searches and provide a search string for the topic 'effects of social media on teen mental health'.
questions.answer: AND requires both terms to appear, narrowing results. OR allows either term, broadening results. NOT excludes a term. A search string: ('social media' OR Instagram OR TikTok) AND ('mental health' OR depression OR anxiety) AND (teenager OR adolescent OR teen) NOT adults. This combines synonyms with OR, requires all concept groups with AND, and excludes irrelevant results with NOT.

questions.question: Read the following passage and write both an acceptable paraphrase and an example of patchwriting. Explain why the patchwriting version is problematic. Original: 'The algorithm creates a feedback loop that progressively narrows the user's information diet, reinforcing existing beliefs while excluding contradictory evidence.'
questions.answer: Acceptable paraphrase: According to the author, recommendation systems trap users in a cycle where they encounter only viewpoints they already hold, because each interaction teaches the algorithm to show more of the same content (Author, 2023). Patchwriting: The algorithm makes a feedback loop that gradually narrows the user's information intake, reinforcing current beliefs while removing contradictory evidence. The patchwriting is problematic because it preserves the original sentence structure and merely swaps individual words with synonyms. True paraphrasing requires restructuring the idea in your own words and sentence patterns.

rubric.key: Patchwriting example clearly shows the problem
Explains why patchwriting is academic dishonesty

questions.question: Format the following source as both an APA and MLA citation. Book: 'Digital Minimalism' by Cal Newport, published in 2019 by Portfolio/Penguin in New York.
questions.answer: APA: Newport, C. (2019). Digital minimalism. Portfolio/Penguin. MLA: Newport, Cal. Digital Minimalism. Portfolio/Penguin, 2019. Key differences: APA uses initials for first name and includes year after author; MLA uses full first name and places year at the end. APA italicizes the title in sentence case; MLA italicizes in title case.

major_assignments.title: Research-Based Argumentative Essay
major_assignments.description: Write a research-based argumentative essay (1500-2000 words) on a topic of your choice related to technology and society. The essay must present a clear thesis, integrate at least 6 scholarly sources, address counterarguments, and follow APA or MLA citation format consistently.
objectives: Demonstrate the complete academic writing process from research to revision
Integrate scholarly sources using summary, paraphrase, and quotation
deliverables: Annotated bibliography of at least 8 potential sources (due week 3)
Peer review feedback form for one classmate's draft
Final revised essay with Works Cited/References page (due week 5)
Revision memo explaining changes made from first to final draft

criteria: Clear, specific, arguable thesis statement
Logical organization supporting the thesis
Effective counterargument and rebuttal
Strong conclusion that extends beyond summary
At least 6 scholarly sources integrated
Sources support rather than replace the student's argument
Effective use of summary, paraphrase, and quotation
Source material analyzed, not just reported
Consistent APA or MLA format throughout
All source material properly cited in-text
Correctly formatted Works Cited or References page
No instances of missing attribution
Clear, concise academic prose
Effective paragraph structure with topic sentences
Smooth transitions between ideas
Error-free grammar, spelling, and punctuation
Compelling, well-supported argumentative essay demonstrating college-level research and writing
Scholarly sources skillfully integrated to support an original argument
Flawless citation format and academic integrity
Clear evidence of substantive revision from first draft to final version

essays.description: Three major essays demonstrating progressive mastery of academic writing

quizzes.description: Regular assessment of writing concepts, research skills, and citation knowledge

peer_review_participation.description: Quality of feedback provided in peer review workshops

research_portfolio.description: Annotated bibliographies, search logs, and source evaluation exercises

final_reflective_essay.description: Reflective essay analyzing growth as a writer over the semester

course_policies.attendance: This is a workshop-based course. Missing class means missing peer review and in-class writing that cannot be replicated. More than 3 absences will lower your grade by 5% per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Drafts and final essays lose 10% per day late. Peer review deadlines are firm because other students depend on your feedback.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review is collaborative. All written work must be your own. You may visit the Writing Center for tutoring but must disclose this on your submission.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism of any kind, including patchwriting and undisclosed AI-generated text, results in a zero on the assignment and a report to the Dean of Students.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Word processor with track changes capability, university library database access, and citation management software (Zotero recommended, free).
